insights.name: Proof Instrumentation
insights.rule: When the hook states a measurable claim (weight, time, money, steps), cut to an extreme close-up of the instrument displaying that number within the first second.
insights.why: Numbers in audio create curiosity, but seeing the physical proof on a screen or scale converts the claim into irrefutable evidence, forcing the viewer to stay for the story behind the metric.

insights.name: Epic-Mundane Gap
insights.rule: State a grand, intellectual, or impossible premise while showing B-roll of an ordinary physical action: walking down stairs, opening a door, or stepping over a curb.
insights.why: The gap between an epic audio claim and a grounded visual creates a curiosity void; the viewer stays to learn how this normal person or setting connects to the extraordinary statement.

insights.name: Contradiction Undercut
insights.rule: Use audio that suggests something is easy, fun, or expected, then immediately undercut it with a visual or on-screen text that shows pain, failure, or danger.
insights.why: Cognitive dissonance arrests the swipe reflex; the brain must resolve the mismatch between the optimistic audio and the negative visual, holding attention through the dissonance.

insights.name: Before-State Setup
insights.rule: Announce a challenge, transformation, or experiment while the visual shows the creator in a casual, unprepared state at home or in everyday clothes.
insights.why: Establishing a relatable 'before' state creates a clear narrative trajectory; viewers commit to watching the gap between normalcy and the promised transformation.

insights.name: Prop Close-Up on Trigger Word
insights.rule: On the first mention of a provocative noun (a price, a ban, a brand, an exotic item), cut immediately to a tight close-up of that object filling most of the frame.
insights.why: Trigger words spike auditory curiosity; delivering an immediate visual payoff satisfies the spike while anchoring the viewer's attention to the central artifact of the video.

insights.name: Aftermath Body State
insights.rule: Tease an extreme result with audio, but show the subject's physical aftermath—sweat, exhaustion, transformed body, or emotional exit—before explaining the cause.
insights.why: Showing the bodily evidence of an event before the story creates a reverse-engineering mystery; viewers stay to learn what could have caused this visible state.

insights.name: Text Overlay Escalation
insights.rule: Layer on-screen text that intensifies, contradicts, or adds consequence to the audio (e.g., audio says 'easy,' text says 'Extremely painful').
insights.why: Dual-channel information delivery increases cognitive load; the brain processes both audio and text simultaneously, deepening engagement and preventing passive scrolling.

insights.name: Direct-Address Dare
insights.rule: Deliver a challenge, bet, or call-to-action as a medium close-up of the creator speaking directly to the camera in an authentic, unpolished room.
insights.why: Direct eye contact combined with a dare triggers a parasocial response; it feels like a personal invitation or confrontation, making the swipe feel like a social rejection.

insights.name: Process Over Product
insights.rule: When introducing a tool or gadget, show a hand interacting with it—putting it on, holding it, adjusting it—rather than a static beauty shot.
insights.why: Human interaction implies imminent action; static product shots read as advertisements, whereas process shots read as demonstrations, promising immediate payoff.

insights.name: Social Ensemble Reveal
insights.rule: For hooks involving group experiments or social dynamics, open with a wide shot that shows the full cast and setting while the premise is stated.
insights.why: Viewers instantly assess the stakes and relationships by seeing who is involved; revealing the ensemble answers 'who?' before the viewer decides to swipe away.

insights.name: Reverse Chronology
insights.rule: Start with the end-state visual (walking away, silhouette, mirror selfie, empty room) while the audio says 'let me explain' or 'I just failed.'
insights.why: Beginning at the end creates an immediate narrative question; the viewer must watch the middle to understand how the story arrived at this visual conclusion.

insights.name: Split-Screen Complexity
insights.rule: When promising intensity, a duel, or multi-part conflict, use a split screen so the visual density matches the audio's promise of simultaneous action.
insights.why: A split frame signals that too much is happening for a single perspective; the visual complexity validates the audio claim of intensity and demands full attention.

insights.name: Aspirational Landscape
insights.rule: Pair self-improvement, qualification, or quest audio with a wide shot of the subject against a vast, extreme, or scenic environment.
insights.why: The landscape visually externalizes the internal journey; the epic setting makes the abstract challenge feel cinematic and worth the viewer's time investment.

insights.name: Visual Escalation
insights.rule: When introducing a material or concept, show it in an unexpected, exaggerated, or bizarre application (e.g., 'this is slime' inside a shoe) rather than its normal form.
insights.why: Escalating the visual beyond the audio's literal description creates a surprise loop; the viewer stays to understand why the familiar item is being used in an insane way.
